photonics.label: Photonics
photonics.subtitle: Optical Interconnects
photonics.tooltip: Light-based data transmission inside AI data centers. Copper cables cannot handle 100kW+ GPU rack bandwidth — optical interconnects replace them.
photonics.investorAngle: Copper hits a physical wall in dense GPU racks, so optical is a forced upgrade — watch transceiver volumes and design wins at the major GPU vendors.

memory.label: AI Memory
memory.subtitle: HBM & DRAM
memory.icon: <rect x="5" y="5" width="14" height="5" rx="1"/><rect x="5" y="14" width="14" height="5" rx="1"/><path d="M8 10v4M12 10v4M16 10v4"/>
memory.tooltip: High-Bandwidth Memory (HBM) is physically stacked on top of NVDA GPUs. Every H100/B200 chip needs it. More AI training = more HBM demand.
memory.investorAngle: HBM demand scales 1:1 with every GPU shipped — watch HBM pricing, capacity expansions, and which suppliers qualify for the newest chips.

fabs.label: Fabs & Foundries
fabs.subtitle: Chip Manufacturing
fabs.tooltip: The factories that physically manufacture chips. NVDA designs GPUs but does not make them — TSMC and others do the actual fabrication.
fabs.investorAngle: NVIDIA can't ship a chip without a fab slot, so leading-edge capacity is the real bottleneck — watch utilization, new node ramps, and pricing power.

neoclouds.label: Neoclouds
neoclouds.subtitle: GPU Cloud Providers
neoclouds.tooltip: New cloud companies built purely for AI GPU compute. They buy NVDA GPUs in bulk and rent compute time to AI startups and enterprises.
neoclouds.investorAngle: A demand signal, not a supplier — heavy GPU buying here confirms compute appetite, but watch their debt loads and customer concentration.

materials.label: Materials & Packaging
materials.subtitle: Substrates & Test
materials.tooltip: Specialty substrates, wafer materials, and laser tools needed to build and package chips. The often-overlooked picks-and-shovels of AI.
materials.investorAngle: Classic picks-and-shovels: these sell into every chip regardless of who wins — watch order growth and exposure to advanced-packaging ramps.

networking.label: Networking
networking.subtitle: AI Cluster Connectivity
networking.tooltip: InfiniBand and Ethernet fabric connecting thousands of GPUs into one coherent cluster. Without this, individual GPUs cannot work together at scale.
networking.investorAngle: Clusters are only as fast as the fabric linking them — watch the InfiniBand-vs-Ethernet share shift and attach rates as cluster sizes grow.

glass.label: Glass Substrates
glass.subtitle: Advanced Packaging Substrates
glass.icon: <rect x="5" y="3" width="14" height="18" rx="1"/><path d="M8 7h8M8 17h8"/><path d="M12 3v18"/>
glass.tooltip: Glass-core substrates for advanced chip packaging (SKC Absolics, TSMC CoPoS). A next-gen substrate layer ramping H2 2026 onward.
glass.investorAngle: An early, pre-revenue layer ramping from ~H2 2026 — higher risk/reward; watch qualification milestones and which packagers commit to glass cores.

robotics.label: Robotics
robotics.subtitle: Humanoids & Automation
robotics.tooltip: Humanoid robots and automation as a new AI-compute demand vertical (Tesla Optimus, Unitree). Early-stage but a major future driver.
robotics.investorAngle: An emerging demand vertical, not a near-term driver — treat as optionality; watch unit shipments and whether AI compute per robot actually scales.

accelerators.label: Accelerators
accelerators.subtitle: GPU / AI Chip Designers
accelerators.tooltip: The GPU and AI-accelerator designers the whole supply chain feeds into. NVDA designs the chips; TSMC and others fabricate them.
accelerators.investorAngle: The chip everyone designs around — watch share between NVIDIA and challengers (AMD, custom silicon) and how fast hyperscalers build their own.

hyperscalers.label: Hyperscalers
hyperscalers.subtitle: The compute buyers
hyperscalers.icon: <rect x="4" y="4" width="6" height="16" rx="1"/><rect x="14" y="4" width="6" height="16" rx="1"/><path d="M6 8h2M6 12h2M6 16h2M16 8h2M16 12h2M16 16h2"/>
hyperscalers.tooltip: Meta, Alphabet, Amazon, Microsoft — the capex engines that buy the GPUs and rent the neocloud capacity. Their spend IS the demand signal for everything below.
hyperscalers.investorAngle: Watch quarterly capex guidance and any 'compute constrained' language — hyperscaler budgets are the single input every supplier thesis depends on.

mapIntro: Everything below NVIDIA feeds the AI chips it designs — suppliers at the bottom, the chip in the middle, the buyers on top. Click any layer to see what it does, what to watch as an investor, and the companies tracked there.

glossary.def: High-bandwidth memory — stacked DRAM bonded next to the GPU; the scarcest ingredient in every accelerator.
Co-packaged optics — moving the optical connection onto the chip package itself; the next photonics wave.
Silicon photonics — making optical parts with chip fabs, so light replaces copper between GPUs.
A cloud built almost entirely of GPUs for rent, not a general-purpose hyperscaler.
A layer with so few capable suppliers that demand has nowhere else to go — where pricing power lives.
Assembling multiple dies (GPU + HBM) into one part — often the real bottleneck, not the fab.
This desk's ranking: core = repeat, high-conviction mentions; watch = credible but thinner; radar = one-off name-drops.

zones.label: The chip · NVIDIA + who builds it

center.title: NVIDIA + Hyperscalers
center.subtitle: GPU Designer & Buyers (MSFT, GOOGL, AMZN, META)
center.tooltip: NVIDIA designs the GPUs that power all AI training and inference. Every company in this map either supplies into NVDA chips, connects to them, or runs on top of them.

unsortedCategory.label: Unsorted
unsortedCategory.subtitle: Awaiting classification
unsortedCategory.tooltip: Auto-added tickers ingested from posts that have not been categorized yet — triage with review.py.
